refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional elements. The refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level outcome dependent on multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, idle management, load optimization). The requirement spans both operational behavior (how the vehicle is driven) and design behavior (how subsystems are architected). High safety criticality tailoring means energy efficiency measures must not compromise safety interlocks or emergency response.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a test protocol that measures total energy consumption (fuel, electrical) and total mass moved over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 8-hour shift, standardized load cycles). Calculate energy_per_ton and compare against E_target. Stratify results by scenario (standard haul, steep grade, tele-operated mode, idle periods) to identify which subsystems dominate energy consumption and where optimization is most effective.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle propulsion system shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and motion.
FR-EE-002: The vehicle shall optimize engine/motor operating point to reduce idle and part-load inefficiency.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, processing, redundancy) while maintaining safety and control responsiveness.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility to operators and remote monitors to enable behavioral optimization.
FR-EE-005: The safety services subsystem shall enforce energy-efficiency-compatible operational policies without compromising safety criticality.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system that monitors real-time fuel/electrical consumption, predicts energy_per_ton trajectory, and provides feedback to operators and autonomous controllers to adjust speed, load, and idle behavior to stay within E_target.

DP-B: Optimize vehicle drivetrain efficiency through high-efficiency transmission, engine tuning, and load-matching algorithms; couple with operator training and incentive policies to reduce wasteful driving patterns.

DP-C: Decompose energy efficiency into subsystem targets (propulsion efficiency ≥ X%, teleoperation overhead ≤ Y%, idle loss ≤ Z%) and allocate design effort to each subsystem independently; integrate via system-level energy accounting.

DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining real-time energy feedback (DP-A), drivetrain optimization (DP-B), and subsystem-level targets (DP-C) with adaptive control that prioritizes safety constraints and adjusts energy targets dynamically based on worksite conditions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid energy management architecture that integrates (1) real-time energy consumption monitoring and feedback to operators/remote monitors, (2) optimized vehicle drivetrain and engine operating point control, (3) subsystem-level energy targets with safety-constrained allocation, and (4) adaptive control logic that adjusts energy optimization strategies based on worksite scenario, load, and safety state. The system shall maintain energy_per_ton ≤ E_target as a monitored KPI while ensuring all safety interlocks and emergency response capabilities remain uncompromised.

potential_sources: Energy optimization logic may couple with safety services if emergency braking or load-drop procedures consume disproportionate energy; mitigation: define safety-critical energy budgets separately and exclude emergency events from E_target calculation.

Teleoperation overhead (communication latency, redundancy) may couple with energy efficiency if bandwidth or processing is increased to improve control responsiveness; mitigation: design teleoperation subsystem to minimize energy footprint while meeting latency and safety requirements.
Operator behavior (training, incentives) couples with technical measures; mitigation: treat behavioral optimization as a separate DP stream and integrate via feedback mechanisms.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite design that decouples energy optimization into independent levers (drivetrain, feedback, subsystem targets, adaptive control) while maintaining integration via a central energy accounting and feedback loop. This reduces risk of over-coupling compared to single-lever approaches (DP-A or DP-B alone).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Real-time energy consumption can be measured accurately via fuel flow sensors, electrical power monitors, and integrated data logging. Risk: Sensor drift or calibration errors could invalidate E_target verification; mitigation: implement sensor health monitoring and periodic calibration protocols.

Assumption: Subsystem-level energy targets can be derived from E_target without over-constraining individual subsystems. Risk: Aggressive subsystem targets may force suboptimal local designs; mitigation: use sensitivity analysis and iterative refinement to balance subsystem targets.

Assumption: Adaptive control logic can adjust energy strategies without introducing latency or instability. Risk: Feedback loops may oscillate or respond too slowly to worksite changes; mitigation: design control law with proven stability margins and test under representative scenarios.

Assumption: Safety criticality can be maintained while implementing energy optimization. Risk: Energy-saving measures (e.g., reduced redundancy, lower idle power) may degrade safety margins; mitigation: conduct FMEA on energy optimization features and define safety-critical energy budgets that are inviolable.

Assumption: Operator training and incentive policies will be adopted and sustained. Risk: Behavioral change may not persist or may be inconsistent across operators; mitigation: embed energy feedback into vehicle UI and teleoperation interface to provide continuous reinforcement.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly separate safety-critical energy budgets (e.g., emergency braking, load-drop, redundancy) from optimization targets. Recommend adding a constraint: 'Energy optimization shall not reduce safety-critical system energy reserves below defined minimums.' This ensures the DP is safety-compatible and reduces coupling risk.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. At this level, the IM assignment for CR-NGHE-003 (Energy efficiency KPI) is the system-level energy management and monitoring capability that spans all subsystems. The NGHE Solution IM owns the energy accounting, KPI calculation (energy_per_ton), feedback mechanisms, and adaptive control orchestration that coordinates subsystem-level energy optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (fuel flow, electrical power, cumulative energy used, energy_per_ton KPI, subsystem energy breakdowns) streamed from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to remote monitoring and analytics platforms. Used by Site Manager and Maintenance/Quality for performance tracking and optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operational directives and energy targets from Site Manager or autonomous planning system. Includes worksite scenario classification, load specifications, and E_target adjustments based on customer requirements or worksite conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services subsystem signals safety state (e.g., emergency mode, load-drop event, reduced-redundancy state) to energy management logic. Energy management may adjust optimization strategies in response to safety state. Safety Services receives energy-related alerts (e.g., energy budget exceeded, subsystem efficiency degraded) that may indicate safety-relevant faults.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication channel for remote monitoring, operator feedback, and adaptive control updates. Energy management logic may adjust strategies based on network latency or bandwidth constraints (e.g., reduce teleoperation overhead if bandwidth is limited).

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level boundary for energy management and KPI calculation. Energy accounting, feedback orchestration, and adaptive control coordination are inherently system-level concerns that span Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Minimal diffusion risk because energy management logic is centralized at the system level. Minor diffusion: subsystem-level energy optimization algorithms (e.g., engine tuning, teleoperation overhead reduction) are owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation modules respectively, but coordinated by NGHE Solution.

Vehicle module owns propulsion efficiency, engine operating point optimization, and drivetrain design (DP-B elements). However, energy_per_ton KPI calculation and system-level feedback require integration across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. If energy management is assigned solely to Vehicle, it would require Vehicle to own interfaces to Teleoperation and Safety Services, creating diffusion and tight coupling. Vehicle should own subsystem-level energy optimization but not system-level KPI management.

Teleoperation module owns energy overhead reduction (DP-C element: teleoperation overhead ≤ Y%). However, teleoperation is only one contributor to energy_per_ton. System-level KPI calculation and feedback require integration across all subsystems. Assigning energy management to Teleoperation would create diffusion and require Teleoperation to coordinate with Vehicle and Safety Services, which is architecturally awkward.

Safety Services owns safety-critical energy budgets and constraints (e.g., emergency braking energy, redundancy overhead). However, Safety Services should not own system-level energy optimization or KPI calculation, as this would couple safety logic with performance optimization and create diffusion. Safety Services should provide constraints and alerts to energy management, not own the management logic itself.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of system-level energy management and KPI calculation because: (1) energy_per_ton is a system-level KPI that requires integration of data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) adaptive control and feedback orchestration are inherently system-level concerns; (3) NGHE Solution already owns system-level interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) that are required for energy management; (4) this assignment minimizes diffusion and coupling by centralizing energy management logic at the system level while allowing subsystems to own their respective energy optimization algorithms.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution module may become a 'god object' if it accumulates too many system-level concerns (energy management, safety coordination, teleoperation orchestration, etc.). Mitigation: Define clear boundaries for NGHE Solution responsibilities. Energy management should focus on KPI calculation, feedback, and adaptive control orchestration. Subsystem-level optimization (propulsion efficiency, teleoperation overhead reduction) should remain owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.

Risk: Energy management logic may require real-time feedback and control loops that demand low-latency communication between NGHE Solution and subsystems. If subsystems are distributed or remote, latency may degrade control responsiveness. Mitigation: Design energy management with asynchronous feedback and adaptive control that tolerates latency. Implement local energy optimization algorithms in Vehicle and Teleoperation that operate independently and are coordinated by NGHE Solution at a slower timescale.

Risk: Safety-critical energy budgets (e.g., emergency braking energy) must be protected from optimization logic. If NGHE Solution owns both energy optimization and safety constraints, there is risk of inadvertent coupling or conflict. Mitigation: Implement safety-critical energy budgets as hard constraints in NGHE Solution energy management logic. Define clear separation between safety-critical and optimization-eligible energy budgets. Conduct FMEA to verify that energy optimization cannot compromise safety.

Risk: Subsystem-level energy targets (DP-C element) may conflict with system-level E_target if subsystems are over-constrained or under-constrained. Mitigation: Use sensitivity analysis and iterative refinement to derive subsystem targets from E_target. Implement feedback mechanism in NGHE Solution to adjust subsystem targets if system-level KPI is not being met.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary for system-level energy management. Recommend refining the scope to explicitly separate: (1) System-level energy accounting and KPI calculation (owned by NGHE Solution), (2) Subsystem-level energy optimization algorithms (owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation), (3) Safety-critical energy budgets and constraints (owned by Safety Services, enforced by NGHE Solution). This separation reduces diffusion and clarifies ownership boundaries. Additionally, recommend defining a formal interface contract between NGHE Solution and subsystems for energy data exchange (e.g., real-time power consumption, subsystem efficiency metrics) to enable effective feedback and adaptive control.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid energy management architecture) directly addresses the refined FR by providing mechanisms to monitor, control, and optimize energy_per_ton across all subsystems while maintaining safety constraints. The DP includes real-time feedback (FR verification), drivetrain optimization (FR achievement), subsystem-level targets (FR decomposition), and adaptive control (FR robustness). The DP is feasible, testable, and compatible with the high safety criticality tailoring. No fundamental mismatch between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Subsystem-level energy optimization algorithms (engine tuning, teleoperation overhead reduction) are distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, but coordinated by NGHE Solution. This is acceptable because subsystems own their local optimization logic, and NGHE Solution owns the system-level orchestration. However, if subsystem algorithms are not well-defined or if interfaces are unclear, diffusion could increase. Mitigation: Define clear API contracts for subsystem energy data and optimization parameters.

Diffusion Risk 2: Safety-critical energy budgets are owned by Safety Services but enforced by NGHE Solution energy management logic. This creates a dependency between Safety Services and NGHE Solution. If safety constraints are not clearly communicated or if energy management logic inadvertently violates them, safety could be compromised. Mitigation: Implement safety-critical energy budgets as hard constraints in NGHE Solution logic. Conduct formal verification that energy optimization cannot violate safety constraints.

Diffusion Risk 3: Operator behavior (training, incentives) is a key element of DP-D but is not directly owned by any technical module. This creates diffusion across organizational and technical boundaries. Mitigation: Define behavioral optimization as a separate workstream and integrate via feedback mechanisms in the vehicle UI and teleoperation interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for system-level energy management. The assignment minimizes diffusion by centralizing KPI calculation, feedback, and adaptive control orchestration at the system level, while allowing subsystems to own their respective optimization algorithms. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and support the required data flows for energy management. No new interfaces are required.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable through a test protocol that measures energy_per_ton over a defined evaluation period and compares against E_target. The NGHE Solution IM provides the necessary infrastructure (energy accounting, KPI calculation, data logging) to support this verification. Verification can be conducted in the field under representative worksite conditions. Feasibility is high.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The refined FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the system-level energy management owner, with sub-FRs allocated to Vehicle (propulsion efficiency, engine optimization), Teleoperation (overhead reduction), and Safety Services (safety-compatible energy constraints). This allocation is clear and traceable. Each sub-FR can be further decomposed and allocated to specific subsystem modules in subsequent decomposition levels.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine DP-D to explicitly define the adaptive control logic and the conditions under which energy optimization strategies are adjusted. Include decision rules for prioritizing safety constraints over energy optimization.

Revision 2: Define formal interface contracts between NGHE Solution and subsystems for energy data exchange (e.g., real-time power consumption, subsystem efficiency metrics, energy budget status). This will reduce ambiguity and support effective integration.
Revision 3: Conduct FMEA on energy optimization features to identify failure modes that could compromise safety or energy efficiency. Document mitigation strategies for high-risk failure modes.

Revision 4: Define a test and evaluation protocol that specifies how energy_per_ton will be measured, what scenarios will be tested, and how results will be validated against E_target. Include acceptance criteria and success metrics.

decision.justification: The refined FR, selected DP, and assigned IM are sound and pass all gate reviews. The FR is decomposed into five sub-FRs that address distinct aspects of energy efficiency (propulsion, engine optimization, teleoperation overhead, visibility, safety compatibility). The DP is a composite design that integrates multiple optimization levers while maintaining safety constraints. The IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate and minimizes diffusion. However, the sub-FRs are not yet atomic and require further decomposition to define specific design parameters and implementation modules for each subsystem. Recommend proceeding to the next decomposition level to refine sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) and allocate them to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle propulsion system shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and motion.
FR-EE-002: The vehicle shall optimize engine/motor operating point to reduce idle and part-load inefficiency.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, processing, redundancy) while maintaining safety and control responsiveness.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility to operators and remote monitors to enable behavioral optimization.
FR-EE-005: The safety services subsystem shall enforce energy-efficiency-compatible operational policies without compromising safety criticality.

assumptions: Real-time energy consumption can be measured accurately via fuel flow sensors, electrical power monitors, and integrated data logging.
Subsystem-level energy targets can be derived from E_target without over-constraining individual subsystems.
Adaptive control logic can adjust energy strategies without introducing latency or instability.
Safety criticality can be maintained while implementing energy optimization.
Operator training and incentive policies will be adopted and sustained.
Worksite scenarios can be classified and used to adjust energy optimization strategies.
Energy_per_ton KPI is a valid and sufficient metric for energy efficiency performance.
Safety-critical energy budgets can be defined and protected from optimization logic.
open_risks: Sensor drift or calibration errors could invalidate E_target verification.
Aggressive subsystem targets may force suboptimal local designs.
Feedback loops may oscillate or respond too slowly to worksite changes.
Energy-saving measures may degrade safety margins.
Behavioral change may not persist or may be inconsistent across operators.
Subsystem-level energy targets may conflict with system-level E_target.
Diffusion of subsystem-level optimization algorithms across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.
Coupling between Safety Services and NGHE Solution energy management logic.
Organizational and technical boundaries for behavioral optimization.
Real-time latency in energy feedback and adaptive control loops.
evidence_needed: Sensor specifications and calibration procedures for energy measurement.
Sensitivity analysis and iterative refinement results for subsystem energy targets.
Control law design and stability analysis for adaptive energy management.
FMEA results for energy optimization features and safety impact assessment.
Operator training and incentive policy design and effectiveness metrics.
Worksite scenario classification and energy optimization strategy mapping.
Test and evaluation protocol for energy_per_ton measurement and E_target verification.
Interface contracts between NGHE Solution and subsystems for energy data exchange.
Formal verification that energy optimization cannot violate safety constraints.
Latency analysis and performance testing for energy feedback and adaptive control loops.
